records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Threatened (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Threatening.]
usage: -- Threaten, Menace. Threaten is Anglo-Saxon, and menace is Latin. As often happens, the former is the more familiar term; the latter is more employed in formal style. We are threatened with a drought; the country is menaced with war.

defs: To utter threats against; to menace; to inspire with apprehension; to alarm, or attempt to alarm, as with the promise of something evil or disagreeable; to warn.
quotes: Let us straitly threaten them, that they speak henceforth to no man in this name.

defs: To exhibit the appearance of (something evil or unpleasant) as approaching; to indicate as impending; to announce the conditional infliction of; as, to threaten war; to threaten death.
quotes: The skies look grimly And threaten present blusters.
By turns put on the suppliant and the lord: Threatened this moment, and the next implored.
Of the sharp ax Regardless, that o'er his devoted head Hangs menacing.

defs: To use threats, or menaces; also, to have a threatening appearance.
quotes: Though the seas threaten, they are merciful.
